summary_text:
Chief Complaint
Evaluation for hematuria and follow-up of previously identified kidney stone.

History of Present Illness
The patient reports experiencing episodes of bright red urine, resembling cherry kool-aid, for the past six weeks, following the passage of two small stones last week. He also reported constant urination and severe pain last week. He rates the pain at the time as an eight on a scale of zero to ten. He reports drinking primarily Mountain Dew and admits to inadequate water intake, estimating approximately 125 ounces daily based on his 250-pound weight. He also consumes foods high in sodium, oxalate, and animal protein. He is taking Alavert 10 milligrams daily and has no known drug allergies.

Findings
The patient's urine dipstick revealed no leukocytes, nitrates, protein, ketone, or bilirubin. The urine color was yellow and clear, with urobilinogen at 0.2 and a pH of 5.5, and a specific gravity of 1.020. A trace amount of blood was observed. Renal ultrasound revealed a right kidney measuring 10 cm by 5 cm, with no stones, masses, or hydronephrosis. The left kidney measures 10.8 cm by 5 cm and contains a 6 mm non-obstructing stone. Pre-void volume was 533 mL and post-void volume was 0 mL. Physical exam revealed no flank pain or tenderness. The abdominal exam was unremarkable. A normal vaginal exam was performed.

Assessment
Chronic urolithiasis with hematuria. No acute obstruction or pain.

Plan
I do not recommend surgical intervention at this time given the absence of obstruction or pain. I recommend drinking approximately 125 ounces of water daily to aid in stone passage and avoiding sugary drinks like Mountain Dew. I recommend limiting sodium intake to less than 1000 mg per day and reducing consumption of beef, poultry, fish, and pork. I have provided a handout detailing dietary recommendations and foods to avoid. Follow up in one year unless there are recurrent episodes of bleeding or pain, at which point you can contact the office sooner. If you experience severe pain or bright red blood in your urine, please go to the emergency room.
reference_summary:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Hematuria.
Chronic urolithiasis.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mrs. Kelly Wood is a 41-year-old female established patient with chronic urolithiasis who returns in follow up for renal ultrasound due to hematuria.

The patient reports having intermittent episodes of gross hematuria over the past several months. she states she had multiple voids in one day where the color of her urine was like cherry Kool-Aid last week; then passed 2 small stone like structures. Pain at the time was 8 out of 10 on a scale of 10. she currently denies any flank pain.

CURRENT MEDICATIONS

Alavert 10 mg tablet once daily.

ALLERGIES

No known drug allergies.

PHYSICAL EXAM

Genitourinary: Normal vaginal exam.

RESULTS

Renal Ultrasound
Impression: Right kidney is 10 cm in length by 5 cm wide. There are no stones, masses, or hydronephrosis. The left kidney is 10.8 cm in length and 5 cm wide. There is a 6 mm left renal stone, non-obstructing. 533 ml pre void and 0 ml post void.

Urine Dipstick without Micro
Color: Yellow.
Appearance: Clear.
Leukocytes: Negative.
Nitrate: Negative.
Urobilinogen: 0.2 EU/dL.
Protein: Negative.
pH: 5.5
Blood: Trace, intact.
Specific gravity: 1.020
Ketone: Negative.
Bilirubin: Negative.
Glucose: Negative.

ASSESSMENT

• Hematuria.
• Chronic urolithiasis.

Mrs. Wood presents today for renal ultrasound. The patient has had recurrent episodes of hematuria over the past few months secondary to chronic urolithiasis. Mrs. Wood reports passing two small stone like structures last week. Urinalysis today was notable for only a trace amount of blood and the renal ultrasound revealed a 6 mm non-obstructing renal stone in the left kidney.

PLAN

No surgical intervention is recommended, and the patient would like to discuss urolithiasis prevention. We had a very lengthy discussion with regards to urolithiasis formation and treating the underlying cause to prevent reoccurrence and associated potential dietary factors that could be involved with urolithiasis formation.

It is recommended that the patient maintain a healthy diet; limit salt, and animal protein. she should increase fluid consumption; adequate intake is half of her body weight in ounces of water daily and avoid sugary drinks. A copy of the dietary recommendations and avoidances was provided to the patient for reference. We will continue to monitor annually with renal ultrasound unless new symptoms develop. The patient voiced understanding and agreed with the recommended medical treatment plan.

INSTRUCTIONS

Follow up in 1 year with renal ultrasound, sooner should symptoms arise. Should you develop sudden onset of severe pain or gross hematuria go to the emergency room.

section_miscategorizations.text: A normal vaginal exam was performed.
section_miscategorizations.reason: The content itself (a normal vaginal exam finding) belongs in Findings, but in the context of a male patient with kidney stones and hematuria, this statement is almost certainly erroneous or a templating artifact rather than a true mis-sectioning issue. It does not represent a different section's content, but it could confuse the reader about patient sex and exam relevance.
